title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Philosophy of Boredom
content.subtitle: Why a lack of stimulation might be the ultimate catalyst for creativity.
content.text: In the age of the smartphone, boredom has become a rare commodity. We have at our fingertips an infinite stream of entertainment, information, and social connection that can fill every 'dead moment'—from waiting for a bus to standing in a grocery line. However, psychologists are beginning to warn that by eliminating boredom, we are also eliminating the mental state required for deep creativity. [31] The writer suggests that boredom is not an empty space to be filled, but a 'threshold' that forces the mind to look inward and generate its own stimulation. [/31] When we are bored, our brains enter 'default mode,' a state of wandering that is essential for problem-solving and original thought. || This 'default mode network' is only activated when we are not focused on an external task. It is the period when the brain makes unexpected connections between disparate ideas, the 'Aha!' moments that lead to innovation. [32] By constantly 'scratching the itch' of boredom with digital distractions, we are effectively preventing our minds from reaching these higher levels of cognitive functioning. [/32] We have become addicted to a form of passive consumption that keeps our brains on the surface, never allowing them to dive into the deeper waters of reflection. || [33] The author's tone when discussing the 'over-scheduled child' is one of concerned reflection. [/33] Just as with adults, children are increasingly protected from the experience of 'having nothing to do.' Every hour is filled with structured activities, sports, or screen time. Yet, some of the most important developmental milestones occur during 'unstructured time,' where children are forced to use their imagination to invent games and explore their environment. By removing the possibility of being bored, we may be inadvertently stifling the development of self-reliance and creative agency in the next generation. || [34] Interestingly, some philosophers argue that there is a difference between 'situational boredom' and 'existential boredom.' [/34] Situational boredom is the temporary annoyance of waiting for something, while existential boredom is a deeper, more profound sense of emptiness or lack of purpose. While the former is a useful trigger for creativity, the latter can be a sign of a deeper crisis. However, even existential boredom can be a valuable signal; it is the mind's way of telling us that our current way of living is no longer meaningful and that a change is required. || [35] Despite the benefits of boredom, some critics argue that it is a luxury that few can afford in a hyper-competitive economy. [/35] For many, 'dead time' is actually a time of stress and anxiety about making ends meet, not a time for creative reflection. This suggests that the 'right to be bored' is unevenly distributed across society. For boredom to be a productive force, it requires a foundation of psychological and financial safety. Without that, 'having nothing to do' is not a catalyst for growth, but a source of fear. || Ultimately, we must learn to be comfortable with stillness. [36] The writer concludes that the most innovative thing we can do in a world of constant noise is to occasionally do absolutely nothing. [/36] It is about reclaimed 'the gap'—the silence between thoughts where the new can emerge. By putting down the phone and allowing ourselves to be bored, we are not wasting time; we are cultivating the soil of our own imagination. The next great idea is probably waiting for us in the very moment we are trying so hard to avoid.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the author suggests that boredom is useful because it...
options: A) helps people find the best content on their smartphones.
B) encourages the mind to create its own interest and ideas.
C) is a very pleasant state that everyone should try to reach.
D) allows people to wait for long periods without becoming annoyed.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31] and the idea of the mind looking inward to 'generate its own stimulation.' A is the distraction the author criticizes. C and D are not the focus.

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, the 'default mode network' is activated when we...
options: A) are intensely focused on a difficult external task.
B) use digital devices to solve problems as fast as possible.
C) are not concentrating on any specific activity.
D) are actively trying to think of a new and innovative idea.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32] and the mention that it is only activated when we are 'not focused on an external task.' A and B are the opposite of this state. D is a goal, but the network is a state of 'wandering'.

questions.question: What is the author's concern regarding children and 'unstructured time'?
options: A) That they will become bored and start misbehaving.
B) That they are not being given enough extracurricular activities.
C) That they are losing the chance to develop their own imagination.
D) That they are spending too much time playing games in the real world.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33] and the mention of stifling the development of 'self-reliance and creative agency.' A is a common adult fear that the author implies is secondary. B is the current trend the author criticizes. D is not mentioned.

questions.question: The fourth paragraph describes 'existential boredom' as a...
options: A) temporary annoyance that can be easily solved with a smartphone.
B) signal that a person's life may need a fundamental change.
C) more creative form of boredom than 'situational boredom'.
D) feeling that only occurs in philosophers and scientists.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [34] and the mention of a signal that 'our current way of living is no longer meaningful.' A describes situational boredom. C and D are not stated.

questions.question: What point is made about the 'right to be bored' in the fifth paragraph?
options: A) It is something that everyone in a modern economy possesses equally.
B) It is a dangerous idea that leads to laziness and economic decline.
C) It depends on having a certain level of security and safety.
D) It is only possible for people who do not have any digital distractions.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35] and the mention of requiring 'a foundation of psychological and financial safety.' A is the opposite of the author's point. B and D are not suggested.

questions.question: Which of the following best summarizes the writer's conclusion?
options: A) Doing nothing is a waste of time in a world that requires innovation.
B) We should use our smartphones to fill the gap between our thoughts.
C) We should value moments of stillness as a source of new ideas.
D) The best way to be creative is to avoid boredom at all costs.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36] and the call to 'reclaim the gap.' A and D are the opposite of the conclusion. B is the behavior the author encourages us to stop.
